files.name: camouflage_doctrine.txt
files.content:
DOCTRINE SUMMARY: THE GHOST ARMY
Archived by the Library of Deception, Wing IV - Strategic Illusion

In 1944, an Allied unit of roughly eleven hundred soldiers, many
drawn from art schools and design studios, was tasked with a single
mission: to be seen as a force many times its actual size.

INFLATABLE ARMOR
  Rubber tanks, inflated overnight and deflated by dawn, photographed
  well from reconnaissance altitude and convincing at a distance.

SONIC DECEPTION
  Recordings of tank columns, engine convoys, and bridge-building
  crews, played from concealed speakers audible for miles at night.

RADIO DECEPTION
  Operators impersonated the call signs and traffic patterns of real
  divisions stationed elsewhere, convincing enemy listening posts
  that units were present in places they had already left.

ATMOSPHERE
  Soldiers painted false unit insignia on their own trucks and wore
  the patches of the divisions they were impersonating, so a captured
  soldier or a curious local would tell the same false story.

Archivist's note: the unit's motto, unofficial and unrecorded, was
said to be "playing for keeps." Its greatest success is that its
existence remained classified for over forty years after the war.

files.name: cartographic_forgeries.txt
files.content:
EXHIBIT NOTES: MAPS THAT LIED
Archived by the Library of Deception, Wing III - Forged Objects

Cartographers have inserted fiction into maps for centuries, for
reasons ranging from copyright protection to national pride.

PAPER TOWNS
  Mapmakers invent a town that does not exist, so that if a rival
  map copies it, the theft is provable. Some paper towns were later
  colonized by mistake, becoming real because people believed the map.

SANDY ISLAND
  Charted in the Coral Sea for over a century and shown on marine
  charts and satellite-imagery services alike, until a 2012 survey
  found open ocean where the island was supposed to be.

PHANTOM MOUNTAINS
  Ranges reported by early explorers, sometimes real weather
  phenomena, sometimes icebergs, sometimes nothing at all, copied
  from atlas to atlas long after the "discoverer" left the region.

SOVIET DISTORTION
  For decades, official Soviet civilian maps deliberately shifted
  roads, rivers, and coordinates, a defensive deception intended to
  frustrate foreign targeting and unauthorized travel alike.

Archivist's note: a map is not a photograph of the world. It is an
argument about the world, and every argument can be made in bad faith.

files.name: con_artist_scripts.txt
files.content:
CASE FILE: THE CONFIDENCE GAME
Archived by the Library of Deception, Wing I - Grift & Ruse

Every confidence game, regardless of era or medium, follows the same
five-beat structure. The details change; the beats do not.

BEAT 1 - THE PUT-UP
  The mark is identified and researched. What do they want badly
  enough to lower their guard for?

BEAT 2 - THE PLAY
  Contact is made. It looks accidental. It is not.

BEAT 3 - THE ROPE
  Trust is built through small, verifiable truths. The con artist
  gives something real before asking for anything.

BEAT 4 - THE TALE
  The pitch. A story explaining why extraordinary reward requires
  the mark's participation, money, or silence, right now.

BEAT 5 - THE CONVINCER
  Early "proof" that the tale is working, usually a small early
  payout, designed to remove the mark's remaining doubt.

Archivist's note: the confidence game is named for what it exploits,
not what it grants. It runs on the mark's confidence, not the con
artist's. Suspicion is the only known countermeasure.

files.name: propaganda_techniques.txt
files.content:
FIELD NOTES: SEVEN PROPAGANDA TECHNIQUES
Archived by the Library of Deception, Wing II - Persuasion & Power

1. NAME CALLING
   Attach a negative label to an idea or person so the audience rejects
   it without examining the evidence. The label does the arguing.

2. GLITTERING GENERALITIES
   The inverse of name calling. Wrap a proposal in warm, vague words
   ("freedom," "progress," "the people") so nobody asks what it means.

3. TRANSFER
   Borrow the authority or prestige of one thing and place it onto
   another. A flag behind a podium. A doctor's coat in an advertisement.

4. TESTIMONIAL
   Let a respected or beloved figure vouch for the claim, so the
   audience's trust in the person transfers to trust in the message.

5. PLAIN FOLKS
   The speaker insists they are "just like you" to disarm suspicion
   of their motives or expertise.

6. CARD STACKING
   Selective use of facts, half-truths, and distraction to build the
   most favorable case possible for an idea, person, or product.

7. BANDWAGON
   "Everyone is doing it." Invite the audience to join the winning
   side rather than evaluate the side on its own merits.

Note from the archivist: none of these techniques require a lie.
The most durable deceptions are built entirely from true statements,
arranged to imply something false.
